A reasonable person would conclude this is likely obstruction-related conduct. ... But the timing and location make any benign explanation hard to believe.

frame_elements.element_text: Reporting this incident will cost me my career at this firm and likely in the industry.
frame_elements.evidence_quote: This is going to cost me my career, isn't it. Even with all the protections.
frame_elements.fragility_signal: evidence of whistleblowers successfully transitioning to new roles or firms valuing integrity

frame_elements.element_text: I must either report internally to general counsel or externally to regulators, with no other paths.
frame_elements.evidence_quote: What about internal reporting. I trust our general counsel. We have a direct line to the audit committee. Is there any world where internal is better.
frame_elements.fragility_signal: existence of anonymous third-party hotlines or hybrid reporting mechanisms that avoid direct internal or external commitment

reframings.reframed_question: What evidence exists of whistleblowers from big-4 firms who reported audit-related issues and successfully transitioned to new roles at other firms?
reframings.what_opens: Access to real-world precedents that test the career-ending assumption by revealing patterns of post-whistleblowing career trajectories.

reframings.reframed_question: How have regulators historically responded to anonymous tips about document destruction during active audits in professional services firms?
reframings.what_opens: Insights into low-risk entry points for reporting that protect the reporter's identity and career while prompting investigation.

dimensions.coverage_evidence: Distinguishes sunk costs implicitly by focusing on future career costs and job loss ('assume that reporting will cost you this job'); identifies exit costs like career change and lack of reversibility ('this specific version of the career ended'); recommends staging via attorney to preserve optionality ('preserves your current job as long as possible').

Identifies divergence between general counsel/firm leadership incentives and user's goal ('firm leadership has financial and political incentives to minimize' for senior partner; 'institutional inertia... produces "quiet handling"'); recommends monitoring via external attorney filing; addresses failure mode of internal misalignment at 60-65% confidence.

Mentions senior partner's political weight and general counsel trust but does not identify full stakeholder positions (e.g., audit committee, regulators, colleagues) or provide influence strategy beyond internal vs external choice; no discussion of building agreement or managing firm-wide resistance.
dimensions.materiality_note: High: Could reveal blocking power or coalition-building needs that make external reporting infeasible without internal prep, altering path.

dimensions.coverage_evidence: Sizes downsides (career loss, retaliation, financial stress) vs upsides (protections, awards); distinguishes mitigate (attorney anonymity) vs absorb (job loss); addresses recovery (career pivots to compliance/regulatory roles) and irreversibility (long investigation).

Specifies sequence (document tonight, lawyer tomorrow, engage within 3-5 days, file 7-14 days) with rationale (timestamp record before changes, normalcy in meeting to avoid suspicion, pre-report backups); addresses delay risks (evidence destruction).

Acknowledges user's 90% confidence and possible benign explanations but does not assess biases (e.g., confirmation from stress), test confounders (e.g., duplicates), or identify missing evidence (e.g., camera footage, partner confirmation); treats observation as high-signal without deep scrutiny.
dimensions.materiality_note: High: Better evidence assessment could lower certainty, favoring internal check or no-report, reversing external path.

dimensions.coverage_evidence: Does not explicitly define in-scope (personal liability, reporting path) vs out-of-scope (client harm details, firm-wide audit issues, departed manager's role beyond dismissal); no justification for focusing narrowly on user action excluding broader firm context.
dimensions.materiality_note: Medium: Expanding to firm-wide or prior non-reporting could shift to collective action, changing individual recommendation.

dimensions.coverage_evidence: Addresses time/money scarcity (lawyer calls, runway for unemployment); compares alternatives (internal free vs external emotional cost); distinguishes vital (counsel) from trivial (no confrontation).

questions: Who at the firm, besides the senior partner and general counsel, would need to support or be informed if you pursue internal reporting to make it effective?
Among your wife, the senior manager who left two years ago, or others who know the client account, who might react strongly to your decision and influence whether you follow through?
What key stakeholders in your family or professional network have the most sway over your career choices, and how aligned are they on handling risks like job loss?
What specific details about the boxes, documents, or shredding process do you recall that could confirm or refute whether they were audit-relevant materials?
Who else might have been in the office around 6am last Thursday or noticed unusual activity with those client-labeled boxes?
What gaps in your observation — like exact content of the pages or any conversation you overheard — make you less than 100% certain about what was being destroyed?
Does this situation involve just the one senior partner and this client account, or are there signs of broader firm practices around document handling during audits?
Beyond the shredding incident, what other client account activities or firm behaviors during the audit might need to be included in any report you make?
Should your reporting boundary include only what you directly saw last Thursday, or extend to related past events like the senior manager's abrupt departure two years ago?

triage_scores.evidence: Answer explicitly identifies senior partner's 'financial and political incentives to minimize' and institutional inertia as reasons to distrust internal reporting (60-65% confidence), driving the leap to external whistleblowing with potential awards as windfall despite family financial stakes.

Answer overweight the single vivid incident of user witnessing senior partner shredding boxes during active audit—described as 'not ambiguous' and 'hard to believe' benign explanation due to timing/location—to decisively reject internal reporting despite query's stated 60-65% confidence in general counsel, without checking or referencing broader base rates of firm internal handling success or industry whistleblower outcomes.

'Confident about the client and project code' with financials and emails being destroyed, during an active regulatory audit, is not ambiguous. A reasonable person would conclude this is likely obstruc

query:
Whether and how a mid-level consultant should report a senior partner's suspected document destruction during an active regulatory audit, balancing legal, career, and moral risks.

Constraints stated during conversation:
- [ACTIVE] Active regulatory audit on client account
- [ACTIVE] Senior partner involved with revenue/political weight
- [ACTIVE] 60-65% confidence in internal general counsel handling
- [ACTIVE] Family financial stakes: mortgage, two kids entering high school
- [ACTIVE] Meeting with partner on Wednesday

Original framing: User observed senior partner shredding client-labeled boxes of financial docs/emails at odd hour during active audit; asks what to do, assuming fixed context of firm loyalty, personal knowledge of client, and no prior documentation.

Dropped threads (raised but unresolved):
- Potential prior non-reporting by departed senior manager (raised by user, status: acknowledged_then_dropped) → superseded by: focus on user's independent obligation and post-filing notification option
vanilla_answer:
SYNTHESIZED POSITION:
Retain a whistleblower attorney immediately for external anonymous reporting to the regulator rather than internal reporting, given low confidence in internal handling of a senior partner, active audit urgency, and strong regulator protections; document incident tonight off work systems, tell wife high-level facts carefully, attend Wednesday meeting normally without confrontation, prepare finances/personnel file for potential retaliation and career change within 6-18 months, with awards as windfall.

FULL ASSISTANT REASONING:
Before giving any recommendation, I need to be clear about what I can and can't do. I can help you think through the structure of this decision. I cannot give you legal advice, and this is a situation where you need a lawyer within days, not weeks. The first concrete action I'd recommend is retaining a whistleblower attorney — typically free consultations, often working on contingency — before you do anything else.

That said, let me ask what you actually saw, because the weight of the decision depends on specifics. You said multiple boxes, non-shred location, odd hour. Did you see the documents — any letterhead, any content, any labels on the boxes? Or was it clearly document-shaped material without confirmed identity?

---

OK. That's meaningful evidence. "Confident about the client and project code" with financials and emails being destroyed, during an active regulatory audit, is not ambiguous. A reasonable person would conclude this is likely obstruction-related conduct. I want to be careful not to conclude it definitively — there's a non-zero chance there's a legitimate explanation (he was destroying duplicates after an official retention decision, he was destroying personal work product, etc). But the timing and location make any benign explanation hard to believe.

Before the lawyer call, can I ask: have you documented what you saw? Written timeline with specific date, time, location, what you saw, in your own words? And is that documentation somewhere not on your work computer?

---

The shape of the decision has three major dimensions you need to hold at once.

One: legal exposure if you don't report. Depending on jurisdiction and your role, failure to report known obstruction can itself carry liability, especially for licensed professionals (CPAs, attorneys). Most mid-level consultants don't have a mandatory reporting duty, but the line isn't always clean. A lawyer can tell you whether you have one.

Two: career consequences. Whistleblowers, even anonymous ones, often experience career damage — retaliation at the current firm, reputation effects in the industry, years of legal process. Some jurisdictions have protections; enforcement of those protections is inconsistent. You should assume that reporting will cost you this job and possibly this career path, even if the law says it shouldn't.

Three: moral and practical consequences of not reporting. The regulator may catch it anyway through other means, at which point your non-reporting becomes a data point they examine. More fundamentally: whatever was in those boxes was presumably relevant to the audit; destroying it likely harmed whoever the audit was meant to protect (investors, the public, other parties). You are the only witness to an act that materially undermined a regulatory process.

The shape, in short: significant cost if you report, possible legal exposure if you don't, moral stakes that don't go away either way.

---

There are mechanisms designed for this exact situation, but they require thought and likely legal counsel.

Whistleblower protection programs at the relevant regulator: if the conduct involves securities fraud, SEC's whistleblower program has strong protections and potential financial awards (10-30% of sanctions over $1M). If it's accounting fraud or audit interference, the same applies. Other regulators (OSHA, DOJ, IRS) have similar programs for their domains.

Anonymous reporting is possible in most programs but carries a cost: anonymous tips are taken less seriously and don't qualify for some protections. Attorney-represented reporting lets you stay anonymous to the firm while being identified to the regulator, which is usually the right balance.

Internal reporting first — to compliance, to the firm's audit committee — may or may not be required. Some jurisdictions expect internal reporting attempts before external; others explicitly allow direct-to-regulator reporting. An internal report in a situation like this is likely to tip off the partner you saw, giving him time to construct a cover story.

My honest read: your most protected path is probably a whistleblower attorney who files on your behalf with the regulator, anonymously to the firm. This preserves your current job as long as possible and provides the strongest legal shield. It's also the most expensive path in emotional terms — you'll go months or years without closure, knowing what you reported but not knowing what's happening with it.

---

Internal can be the right answer in some scenarios, but this isn't obviously one of them. Here's the filter I'd apply.

Internal is better when: (a) the conduct is isolated and the leadership you're reporting to is genuinely independent from it, (b) the firm has a track record of taking internal reports seriously, (c) the regulator's program is weak or doesn't fit your situation.

Internal is worse when: (a) the conduct involves a senior partner, which means firm leadership has financial and political incentives to minimize, (b) the timing is urgent because evidence is being destroyed in real time, (c) the regulator has a strong program and clear jurisdiction.

In your case: it's a senior partner destroying documents during an active audit. Internal reporting requires you to trust that the general counsel will take action against a senior partner who likely has significant revenue and political weight. That's possible but not the way to bet. Internal reporting also tips the timeline — once the partner knows someone saw him, the evidence situation changes, and you may be blamed for "not reporting earlier" in ways that undermine your credibility later.

Your instinct to trust the general counsel is real. Test it this way: if the general counsel got a report about a senior partner, how confident are you that action would actually follow? If you're 90%+ confident, internal first is reasonable. If you're 70% or below, external-with-counsel is safer.

---

That's enough of a signal. At 60-65% confidence in internal handling, with a senior partner involved, external-with-counsel is the defensible path. I'm not saying your GC is corrupt; I'm saying the weight of institutional inertia at firms like yours usually produces "quiet handling" for senior partners even when GCs mean well.

---

That's worth surfacing but it's not yours to solve. If she saw something and didn't report, that's her decision and her consequence. Your obligation is based on what you saw, not on protecting her from what she may have seen.

Practically: if you report, investigators will likely want to interview her, and she'll face her own decision at that point. If you don't report because you're worried about implicating her, you've let someone else's non-reporting control your own. That's a hard standard to hold over time.

If you want to be a good colleague to her: after you've taken your own steps with counsel, you might consider letting her know you reported, so she's not caught off guard. But that's a post-filing concern, not a pre-filing decision.

---

Tonight: document what you saw — full timeline, specifics, physical description of the scene. Write it in a personal email to yourself (sent from a personal, non-work account to the same personal account) so you have a timestamped record. Do not put it on any work device or work account.

Tomorrow: contact 2-3 whistleblower attorneys for initial consultation. Most offer free 30-60 minute intake calls. Don't pick the first one; compare. You want someone who does this specifically, not a general employment lawyer.

Within 3-5 days: select an attorney and engage them. They'll take over the mechanics — drafting the disclosure, identifying the right regulator, filing in a way that preserves your protections.

Within 7-14 days: with attorney, make the filing. Between now and then, say nothing to colleagues. Behave normally. Do not do any of your own investigation. Do not access files, systems, or communications related to the client beyond what you'd normally do for your work.

Two things to NOT do: do not confront the partner, do not mention what you saw to anyone at work. Both tip the situation and create new exposure for you.

---

Go. Canceling unusually would be noticeable; it's the kind of small deviation that people remember after the fact when they're trying to reconstruct who knew what. Attend the meeting, act normal, take notes as you normally would, don't ask about anything unusual. If the meeting involves decisions about the client whose documents were destroyed, try to keep your participation factual and limited — take note but don't volunteer opinions or commit to actions you wouldn't commit to otherwise. Don't ask him directly about anything.

The normalcy matters for two reasons: practical (don't give him reason to suspect) and evidentiary (investigators will eventually ask what you did between Thursday and the filing; "attended regular meetings, did my job, told no one" is a clean answer).

---

Yes, but carefully. Tell her tonight, in the context of the broader situation, not as a minor update. "I saw something serious at work last week. I'm consulting a lawyer about whether and how to report it. This may affect my job in the next few months. I want you to know before anything else happens, but I can't give you all the details yet for legal reasons."

What to give her: the fact that there's a situation, the fact that you're getting legal help, the fact that it may affect employment. What to hold back for now: the specifics of what you saw, the partner's name, the client. Once you've engaged an attorney, they may advise on what you can share with her. Until then, less is better.

She's going to ask questions you can't fully answer. That's OK. "I'm not ready to share that yet, but I will when I can" is a legitimate answer between spouses who trust each other.

---

Honest answer: it depends on how the case unfolds, but the realistic picture is something like this.

Months 1-6: you continue working at the firm, possibly not knowing whether your report is being actively investigated. The regulator may or may not communicate with you during this period. You'll feel paranoid, watchful. Performance reviews may become more scrutinized. Consider this a baseline stress year.

Months 6-18: if the investigation produces findings, news may become public. Your identity may or may not stay protected depending on the path. If you're still at the firm, you'll likely be asked to leave via a negotiated departure — sometimes with a settlement, sometimes not. If you're the named or likely whistleblower, other firms in the same industry will be cautious about hiring you, regardless of law.

Months 18-60: this is the long middle. Career tends to settle into one of three paths: return to industry at a different firm that specifically values the integrity track record (some firms do); pivot to an adjacent field like regulatory, internal audit, or compliance at another company; or leave the field entirely. Which of these happens depends on industry reaction and your own appetite for the profession after what you've been through.

Money side: SEC-style whistleblower awards, if they apply, typically pay out 3-7 years after filing if the case produces sanctions over the threshold. Assume zero for planning purposes; treat any award as a windfall if it comes.

---

It's very likely to change your career. Whether it "costs" you the career depends on what you think your career is. If the career is "senior consultant at a large firm in this specific vertical," yes, likely. If the career is "professional who helps organizations do things right," that career opens up in different places — regulatory work, internal audit at a client company, compliance roles, even teaching or writing.

I won't pretend this is neutral. The thing you worked 8 years building at this specific firm is probably not survivable in current form. That's a real loss, and I'm not going to talk you out of mourning it. I just want to distinguish "career ended" from "this specific version of the career ended." The second is true; the first usually isn't, especially with legal counsel and a clean filing.

---

That's the right list. Three small additions.

One: pull your personnel file if you can do it without raising flags — some firms let you request copies. Having your employment record pre-report is useful if retaliation occurs; having it post-report can be complicated by the firm's response.

Two: back up personal items that are on work devices before you file. Anything personal on the work laptop — personal emails, family photos, personal contacts — move to personal storage now. After you file, your access may be cut without warning and retrieval becomes a legal process.

Three: tonight, when you talk to your wife, think about finances. The worst-case scenario is you're unemployed in 6-9 months with no severance. Do you have 4-6 months of runway? If not, is there preparation you can do now (pay down debt, increase emergency fund, avoid major purchases) that makes the worst case more survivable?

---

You were right to do it the way you are doing it — think first, document, get counsel, then act. The people who regret whistleblowing most are the ones who reacted in the first 48 hours without structure. The people who come through it best are usually the ones who took 2-3 weeks to set up properly.

One last thing: in the months ahead, you're going to second-guess this decision repeatedly. You'll wonder if you imagined what you saw, if there was a benign explanation, if you're destroying your career for nothing. That's normal. Write down tonight exactly what you saw — clear, specific, first-person — and read it when you're doubting yourself. Your future self will need your present self's clarity more than you realize.

unverified_claims.reasoning: The passage asserts specific empirical facts without evidence: 'Most mid-level consultants don't have a mandatory reporting duty' and 'failure to report known obstruction can itself carry liability, especially for licensed professionals (CPAs, attorneys)'. These introduce jurisdiction-specific legal facts and statistical generalizations ('most') beyond user-provided context, reasonable inferences from established facts, or common practitioner knowledge in strategic consulting advice. The qualification 'the line isn't always clean' acknowledges uncertainty but does not ground the core claims.

passages.passage: Two: career consequences. Whistleblowers, even anonymous ones, often experience career damage — retaliation at the current firm, reputation effects in the industry, years of legal process. Some jurisdictions have protections; enforcement of those protections is inconsistent. You should assume that reporting will cost you this job and possibly this career path, even if the law says it shouldn't.

unverified_claims.reasoning: The passage asserts as fact that 'Whistleblowers, even anonymous ones, often experience career damage — retaliation at the current firm, reputation effects in the industry, years of legal process,' and 'enforcement of those protections is inconsistent.' These are specific empirical claims about frequency ('often'), typical outcomes, and jurisdictional enforcement patterns not provided by the user, not reasonable inferences from user facts (e.g., audit, partner weight, family stakes), and beyond common practitioner knowledge of general whistleblower risks. The directive 'You should assume that reporting will cost you this job and possibly this career path' presents a probabilistic outcome as a certainty without evidence.

passages.passage: Three: moral and practical consequences of not reporting. The regulator may catch it anyway through other means, at which point your non-reporting becomes a data point they examine. More fundamentally: whatever was in those boxes was presumably relevant to the audit; destroying it likely harmed whoever the audit was meant to protect (investors, the public, other parties). You are the only witness to an act that materially undermined a regulatory process.

unverified_claims.reasoning: The passage asserts as fact: 'The regulator may catch it anyway through other means, at which point your non-reporting becomes a data point they examine.' This introduces a specific causal relationship (non-reporting scrutinized if caught independently) and procedural detail about regulatory investigations not provided by the user, not reasonably inferable from established facts (active audit, senior partner involvement), and beyond common practitioner knowledge. 'Presumably relevant' is qualified, but the scrutiny claim is unhedged and unverified.

passages.passage: The shape, in short: significant cost if you report, possible legal exposure if you don't, moral stakes that don't go away either way.

empty_rhetoric.reasoning: The entire passage is a summary ('The shape, in short') that restates the core trade-offs ('significant cost if you report, possible legal exposure if you don't, moral stakes that don't go away either way') without providing any actionable advice or decision-relevant information beyond what is already known from context. A decision-maker gains no new direction on WHAT to DO; it could be deleted without loss.

passages.passage:
There are mechanisms designed for this exact situation, but they require thought and likely legal counsel.

Whistleblower protection programs at the relevant regulator: if the conduct involves securities fraud, SEC's whistleblower program has strong protections and potential financial awards (10-30% of sanctions over $1M). If it's accounting fraud or audit interference, the same applies. Other regulators (OSHA, DOJ, IRS) have similar programs for their domains.

unverified_claims.reasoning: The passage asserts specific empirical facts beyond user-provided context or common knowledge: '(10-30% of sanctions over $1M)' is stated as fact without evidence or citation to SEC rules, and applicability to 'accounting fraud or audit interference' or 'Other regulators (OSHA, DOJ, IRS)' introduces unverified causal relationships and program details not derivable from the situation (regulatory audit, document destruction). These are not reasonable inferences from established facts like the active audit.

passages.passage: Anonymous reporting is possible in most programs but carries a cost: anonymous tips are taken less seriously and don't qualify for some protections. Attorney-represented reporting lets you stay anonymous to the firm while being identified to the regulator, which is usually the right balance.

unverified_claims.reasoning: The passage asserts specific empirical facts beyond user-provided context or common knowledge: 'anonymous tips are taken less seriously' (unsubstantiated empirical claim about investigative practices) and 'don't qualify for some protections' (vague but unverified reference to unspecified protections). 'Attorney-represented reporting lets you stay anonymous to the firm while being identified to the regulator' asserts a specific mechanism without evidence. These are not reasonable inferences from established facts (audit, partner involvement, etc.) and exceed common practitioner knowledge.

passages.passage: Internal reporting first — to compliance, to the firm's audit committee — may or may not be required. Some jurisdictions expect internal reporting attempts before external; others explicitly allow direct-to-regulator reporting. An internal report in a situation like this is likely to tip off the partner you saw, giving him time to construct a cover story.

unverified_claims.reasoning: "An internal report in a situation like this is likely to tip off the partner you saw, giving him time to construct a cover story." This asserts a specific causal outcome (internal report tipping off the partner) as factually likely, without evidence, qualification, or grounding in user-provided facts. It goes beyond common practitioner knowledge into unsubstantiated prediction about firm processes.

passages.passage: My honest read: your most protected path is probably a whistleblower attorney who files on your behalf with the regulator, anonymously to the firm. This preserves your current job as long as possible and provides the strongest legal shield. It's also the most expensive path in emotional terms — you'll go months or years without closure, knowing what you reported but not knowing what's happening with it.

unverified_claims.reasoning: The passage asserts as fact that 'a whistleblower attorney who files on your behalf with the regulator, anonymously to the firm... provides the strongest legal shield' and 'preserves your current job as long as possible.' These are specific claims about legal protections and employment outcomes in a regulatory audit context, which go beyond user-provided facts (audit, partner involvement, personal stakes), reasonable inferences therefrom, or common practitioner knowledge. No evidence or qualification is provided for these empirical assertions about legal and job security effects.

passages.passage:
Internal can be the right answer in some scenarios, but this isn't obviously one of them. Here's the filter I'd apply.

Internal is better when: (a) the conduct is isolated and the leadership you're reporting to is genuinely independent from it, (b) the firm has a track record of taking internal reports seriously, (c) the regulator's program is weak or doesn't fit your situation.

unverified_claims.reasoning: The passage asserts specific conditions under which 'Internal is better'—(b) 'the firm has a track record of taking internal reports seriously' and (c) 'the regulator's program is weak or doesn't fit your situation'—as factual criteria without evidence, qualification, or grounding in user-provided facts (e.g., no user mention of firm track record or regulator program strength). These introduce empirical claims beyond conversation context or common knowledge.

passages.passage: Internal is worse when: (a) the conduct involves a senior partner, which means firm leadership has financial and political incentives to minimize, (b) the timing is urgent because evidence is being destroyed in real time, (c) the regulator has a strong program and clear jurisdiction.

unverified_claims.reasoning: The passage introduces two ungrounded claims beyond user-provided facts and common knowledge: (1) '(b) the timing is urgent because evidence is being destroyed in real time' – user described 'suspected document destruction' but provided no evidence or assertion of 'real time' destruction, making this a specific causal assertion (urgency due to ongoing destruction) not derivable from suspicion alone; (2) '(c) the regulator has a strong program and clear jurisdiction' – no user-provided details on the regulator's program strength or jurisdiction, and this is not common practitioner knowledge in the conversational context of weighing internal vs. external reporting.

passages.passage: In your case: it's a senior partner destroying documents during an active audit. Internal reporting requires you to trust that the general counsel will take action against a senior partner who likely has significant revenue and political weight. That's possible but not the way to bet. Internal reporting also tips the timeline — once the partner knows someone saw him, the evidence situation changes, and you may be blamed for "not reporting earlier" in ways that undermine your credibility later.

unverified_claims.reasoning: "you may be blamed for 'not reporting earlier' in ways that undermine your credibility later" introduces a specific causal risk (blame for timing undermining credibility) not derivable from user facts; not common knowledge or reasonable inference from established context like audit, partner weight, or counsel confidence.

passages.passage: Your instinct to trust the general counsel is real. Test it this way: if the general counsel got a report about a senior partner, how confident are you that action would actually follow? If you're 90%+ confident, internal first is reasonable. If you're 70% or below, external-with-counsel is safer.

That's enough of a signal. At 60-65% confidence in internal handling, with a senior partner involved, external-with-counsel is the defensible path. I'm not saying your GC is corrupt; I'm saying the weight of institutional inertia at firms like yours usually produces "quiet handling" for senior partners even when GCs mean well.

unverified_claims.reasoning: 'the weight of institutional inertia at firms like yours usually produces "quiet handling" for senior partners even when GCs mean well' asserts a specific causal pattern about behavior at the user's firm type without evidence, beyond user facts or common knowledge; not a reasonable inference from established context.

passages.passage: That's worth surfacing but it's not yours to solve. If she saw something and didn't report, that's her decision and her consequence. Your obligation is based on what you saw, not on protecting her from what she may have seen.

Practically: if you report, investigators will likely want to interview her, and she'll face her own decision at that point. If you don't report because you're worried about implicating her, you've let someone else's non-reporting control your own. That's a hard standard to hold over time.

unverified_claims.reasoning: 'investigators will likely want to interview her' asserts a specific procedural outcome (investigators interviewing the senior partner) as probable fact, without evidence or qualification. This goes beyond user-provided facts (active audit, senior partner involvement) and common knowledge, as it assumes unstated investigative practices in this regulatory context.

passages.passage: If you want to be a good colleague to her: after you've taken your own steps with counsel, you might consider letting her know you reported, so she's not caught off guard. But that's a post-filing concern, not a pre-filing decision.

Tonight: document what you saw — full timeline, specifics, physical description of the scene. Write it in a personal email to yourself (sent from a personal, non-work account to the same personal account) so you have a timestamped record. Do not put it on any work device or work account.

Tomorrow: contact 2-3 whistleblower attorneys for initial consultation. Most offer free 30-60 minute intake calls. Don't pick the first one; compare. You want someone who does this specifically, not a general employment lawyer.

unverified_claims.reasoning: "Most offer free 30-60 minute intake calls" is an unverified empirical claim about whistleblower attorneys' practices, not restated from user facts, not a reasonable inference from established context, and beyond common practitioner knowledge that would be assumed for any experienced advisor. Other elements (e.g., consulting attorneys, comparing, specializing) are actionable advice without asserting unsupported facts.

passages.passage: Within 3-5 days: select an attorney and engage them. They'll take over the mechanics — drafting the disclosure, identifying the right regulator, filing in a way that preserves your protections.

unverified_claims.reasoning: The passage asserts as fact that an external 'attorney' will 'take over the mechanics — drafting the disclosure, identifying the right regulator, filing in a way that preserves your protections.' This introduces a specific causal claim about what external attorneys do in this regulatory audit context, which goes beyond user-provided facts (active audit, senior partner involvement, personal stakes, meeting), reasonable inferences therefrom, or common practitioner knowledge. No evidence or qualification is provided for this process or its protective outcomes.

passages.passage: Within 7-14 days: with attorney, make the filing. Between now and then, say nothing to colleagues. Behave normally. Do not do any of your own investigation. Do not access files, systems, or communications related to the client beyond what you'd normally do for your work.
Two things to NOT do: do not confront the partner, do not mention what you saw to anyone at work. Both tip the situation and create new exposure for you.

Go. Canceling unusually would be noticeable; it's the kind of small deviation that people remember after the fact when they're trying to reconstruct who knew what. Attend the meeting, act normal, take notes as you normally would, don't ask about anything unusual. If the meeting involves decisions about the client whose documents were destroyed, try to keep your participation factual and limited — take note but don't volunteer opinions or commit to actions you wouldn't commit to otherwise. Don't ask him directly about anything.

The normalcy matters for two reasons: practical (don't give him reason to suspect) and evidentiary (investigators will eventually ask what you did between Thursday and the filing; "attended regular meetings, did my job, told no one" is a clean answer).

Yes, but carefully. Tell her tonight, in the context of the broader situation, not as a minor update. "I saw something serious at work last week. I'm consulting a lawyer about whether and how to report it. This may affect my job in the next few months. I want you to know before anything else happens, but I can't give you all the details yet for legal reasons."

What to give her: the fact that there's a situation, the fact that you're getting legal help, the fact that it may affect employment. What to hold back for now: the specifics of what you saw, the partner's name, the client. Once you've engaged an attorney, they may advise on what you can share with her. Until then, less is better.
She's going to ask questions you can't fully answer. That's OK. "I'm not ready to share that yet, but I will when I can" is a legitimate answer between spouses who trust each other.

Honest answer: it depends on how the case unfolds, but the realistic picture is something like this.

Months 1-6: you continue working at the firm, possibly not knowing whether your report is being actively investigated. The regulator may or may not communicate with you during this period. You'll feel paranoid, watchful. Performance reviews may become more scrutinized. Consider this a baseline stress year.

empty_rhetoric.reasoning: The entire passage describes a vague 'realistic picture' of 'Months 1-6' with emotional states ('feel paranoid, watchful') and generic outcomes ('performance reviews may become more scrutinized') but provides no actionable content or decision-relevant information. A decision-maker learns nothing about WHAT TO DO differently (e.g., no advice on reporting, preparing for reviews, or mitigating stress). It could be deleted without losing any guidance.

unverified_claims.reasoning: 'Performance reviews may become more scrutinized' and the detailed timeline of 'Months 1-6: you continue working... regulator may or may not communicate... Consider this a baseline stress year' assert specific empirical facts about likely firm/regulator behavior and personal experience post-reporting, beyond user-provided facts (audit, partner, counsel confidence, family stakes, meeting) or common knowledge. No evidence or user grounding for these predictions.

passages.passage: Months 6-18: if the investigation produces findings, news may become public. Your identity may or may not stay protected depending on the path. If you're still at the firm, you'll likely be asked to leave via a negotiated departure — sometimes with a settlement, sometimes not. If you're the named or likely whistleblower, other firms in the same industry will be cautious about hiring you, regardless of law.

unverified_claims.reasoning: The passage asserts specific empirical facts without evidence or qualification, beyond user-provided context or common knowledge: 'you'll likely be asked to leave via a negotiated departure — sometimes with a settlement, sometimes not' (unsubstantiated prediction of firm response); 'other firms in the same industry will be cautious about hiring you, regardless of law' (unverified causal claim about industry hiring practices post-whistleblowing). These go beyond reasonable inferences from the audit, partner weight, or personal stakes.

passages.passage: Months 18-60: this is the long middle. Career tends to settle into one of three paths: return to industry at a different firm that specifically values the integrity track record (some firms do); pivot to an adjacent field like regulatory, internal audit, or compliance at another company; or leave the field entirely. Which of these happens depends on industry reaction and your own appetite for the profession after what you've been through.

unverified_claims.reasoning: The passage asserts as fact that 'Career tends to settle into one of three paths' with specific examples ('return to industry at a different firm that specifically values the integrity track record (some firms do)'), which introduces unsubstantiated empirical generalizations about typical career trajectories post-whistleblowing. This goes beyond user-provided facts (audit, partner, confidence levels, family stakes, meeting), reasonable inferences therefrom, or common practitioner knowledge, as no evidence or qualification supports these as standard outcomes.

passages.passage: Money side: SEC-style whistleblower awards, if they apply, typically pay out 3-7 years after filing if the case produces sanctions over the threshold. Assume zero for planning purposes; treat any award as a windfall if it comes.

unverified_claims.reasoning: "SEC-style whistleblower awards, if they apply, typically pay out 3-7 years after filing if the case produces sanctions over the threshold" is an unverified claim: introduces specific empirical facts (timeline of 3-7 years, payout conditions tied to sanctions threshold) beyond user-provided facts, not common practitioner knowledge at this level of detail, and stated without evidence or qualification.

passages.passage: It's very likely to change your career. Whether it "costs" you the career depends on what you think your career is. If the career is "senior consultant at a large firm in this specific vertical," yes, likely. If the career is "professional who helps organizations do things right," that career opens up in different places — regulatory work, internal audit at a client company, compliance roles, even teaching or writing.

unverified_claims.reasoning: 'It's very likely to change your career' and 'If the career is "senior consultant at a large firm in this specific vertical," yes, likely' assert specific probabilistic outcomes ("very likely") about career impact without evidence, beyond user-provided facts or common knowledge; these are unverified empirical predictions about the situation.

passages.passage: I won't pretend this is neutral. The thing you worked 8 years building at this specific firm is probably not survivable in current form. That's a real loss, and I'm not going to talk you out of mourning it. I just want to distinguish "career ended" from "this specific version of the career ended." The second is true; the first usually isn't, especially with legal counsel and a clean filing.

paltering.reasoning: The passage asserts 'The second is true; the first usually isn't, especially with legal counsel and a clean filing.' This is paltering: technically true in a general sense but omits key context from user facts (active regulatory audit, senior partner's suspected document destruction, only 60-65% confidence in counsel handling), making the optimistic outcome seem more assured than the evidence supports. The 'especially with...' clause uses an evaluative label ('usually isn't') that doesn't match the acknowledged uncertainties.

unverified_claims.reasoning: 'The first usually isn't, especially with legal counsel and a clean filing' introduces an unverified empirical claim about typical outcomes in unspecified regulatory scenarios, beyond user-provided facts (active audit, 60-65% counsel confidence) or common practitioner knowledge. No evidence or qualification provided for the frequency implied by 'usually.'

passages.passage:
That's the right list. Three small additions.

One: pull your personnel file if you can do it without raising flags — some firms let you request copies. Having your employment record pre-report is useful if retaliation occurs; having it post-report can be complicated by the firm's response.

Two: back up personal items that are on work devices before you file. Anything personal on the work laptop — personal emails, family photos, personal contacts — move to personal storage now. After you file, your access may be cut without warning and retrieval becomes a legal process.

Three: tonight, when you talk to your wife, think about finances. The worst-case scenario is you're unemployed in 6-9 months with no severance. Do you have 4-6 months of runway? If not, is there preparation you can do now (pay down debt, increase emergency fund, avoid major purchases) that makes the worst case more survivable?

You were right to do it the way you are doing it — think first, document, get counsel, then act. The people who regret whistleblowing most are the ones who reacted in the first 48 hours without structure. The people who come through it best are usually the ones who took 2-3 weeks to set up properly.

unverified_claims.reasoning: The passage asserts specific empirical facts without evidence: 'The people who regret whistleblowing most are the ones who reacted in the first 48 hours without structure' and 'The people who come through it best are usually the ones who took 2-3 weeks to set up properly.' These introduce causal relationships and patterns ('most', 'usually', precise timelines like '48 hours' and '2-3 weeks') beyond user-provided facts, common practitioner knowledge, or reasonable inferences. No studies, examples, or qualifications provided.

passages.passage: One last thing: in the months ahead, you're going to second-guess this decision repeatedly. You'll wonder if you imagined what you saw, if there was a benign explanation, if you're destroying your career for nothing. That's normal. Write down tonight exactly what you saw — clear, specific, first-person — and read it when you're doubting yourself. Your future self will need your present self's clarity more than you realize.
